messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_doc_06f8fa1cac644fe2.pdf
Equipment class: valve

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_c232b516486c58da", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Design outdoor dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures", "summary": "Design outdoor dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures (absolute and coincident) should be included in HVAC&R system program.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "design_outdoor_temperatures", "title": "Design outdoor dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures", "summary": "Design outdoor dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures (absolute and coincident) should be included in HVAC&R system program."}, "evidence": "14⏐ THE DESIGN  PROCESS
The information that should be  contained in the HV AC&R sys-
tem program includes
 design outdoor dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures (absolute
and coincident);
 heating and cooling degree-days/hours;
 design wind velocity (and direction) for winter and summer;
 applicable zoning, building, mechanical, fire, and energy
codes; and
 rate structure, capacity, and characteristics of available utilities
and fuels.
Additional information regardi ng solar radiation availability
and subsurface conditions would be included if use of a solar ther-
mal system or ground-source heat pump was anticipated.
The environmental conditions to be maintained for each build-
ing space should be defined by
 dry-bulb and wet-bulb temperatures during daytime occupied
hours,  nighttime occupied hours, and unoccupied hours;
 ventilation and indoor air quality requirements;
 any special conditions, such as  heavy internal equipment loads,
unusual lighting requirements, noise- and-vibration-free areas,
humidity limits, and redundancy fo r life safety and security; and
 acceptable range of conditions for each of the above.
An understanding of the functional use for each area is essen-
tial to select appropriate HV AC&R systems and suitable control
approaches because the capabilities of proposed systems must be
evaluated and compared to the indoor environmental requirements.
For example, if some rooms in a building require humidity control
while others do not, the HV AC sy stem must be able to provide
humidification to areas requiring it without detriment to the build-
ing enclosure or other spaces.", "source_pages": [1], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_6fe4dd9f3e32f988", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Ventilation rate minimum for gas removal spaces", "summary": "Ventilation rate in spaces with gas removal provisions should be maintained at no less than 15 cfm per person.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "minimum_ventilation_rate_gas_removal", "title": "Ventilation rate minimum for gas removal spaces", "summary": "Ventilation rate in spaces with gas removal provisions should be maintained at no less than 15 cfm per person."}, "evidence": "14⏐ THE DESIGN  PROCESS

ing enclosure or other spaces.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_bfaa9f40c1eb989c", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Maximum air movement for thermal comfort", "summary": "Standard 55 prescribes maximum air speed of 30 fpm for winter and 50 fpm for summer.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "maximum_air_speed_comfort", "title": "Maximum air movement for thermal comfort", "summary": "Standard 55 prescribes maximum air speed of 30 fpm for winter and 50 fpm for summer."}, "evidence": "Notwithstanding such gas removal provi-
sions, the ventilation rate in these types of spaces should be main-
tained at no less than 15 cf m [7.1 L/s] per person (except as
otherwise permitted by Standard 62.1) to limit the carbon dioxide
concentration.
3.4 ROOM AIR DISTRIBUTION
3.4.1 Predicting Thermal Comfort
Standard  55 is based upon the presumption (explicitly stated
in early versions of the standard) that “there is no minimum air
movement that is necessary for thermal comfort,” although this is
contrary to the experience of many building operators (see Section
3.4.2). The standard further prescribes a maximum rate of air
movement (air speed) of 30 fpm [0.15 m/s] for winter and 50 fpm
[0.25 m/s] for summer. Those values essentially agree with the
data found in the “Space Air Diffusion “chapter in the ASHRAE
Handbook—Fundamentals .
That chapter of the Handbook  describes different methods of
room air distribution.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_fbe3828a21d8ddab", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "ADPI prediction applicability temperature", "summary": "ADPI predictions apply only if average space temperature is close to 76°F.", "structured_payload": {"title": "ADPI prediction applicability temperature", "summary": "ADPI predictions apply only if average space temperature is close to 76°F."}, "evidence": "14⏐ THE DESIGN  PROCESS

constant pressure drop. The quick-opening characteristic is usually
applied where a process requires essentially ON/OFF operation. For
example, a preheat coil in an outdoo r air duct may be operated by a
quick-opening valve, the controller of which has a sensor in the out-
door air.
A linear valve gives a uniform percentage change in flow for
each percentage change in valve position. A typical application
would be on a steam-heating coil operated by a controller whose
sensor is on the discharge side of the coil.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_9f737e13804f2a3c", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Linear valve application on steam coil", "summary": "Linear valve is typically applied on a steam-heating coil with discharge sensor.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Linear valve application on steam coil", "summary": "Linear valve is typically applied on a steam-heating coil with discharge sensor."}, "evidence": "104⏐ COMPONENTS
constant pressure drop. The quick-opening characteristic is usually
applied where a process requires essentially ON/OFF operation. For
example, a preheat coil in an outdoo r air duct may be operated by a
quick-opening valve, the controller of which has a sensor in the out-
door air.
A linear valve gives a uniform percentage change in flow for
each percentage change in valve position. A typical application
would be on a steam-heating coil operated by a controller whose
sensor is on the discharge side of the coil.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_84d8954e78015553", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Equal-percentage valve for hydronic coil", "summary": "Equal-percentage valve is used for hydronic heating or cooling coil to counteract nonlinear coil heat transfer.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Equal-percentage valve for hydronic coil", "summary": "Equal-percentage valve is used for hydronic heating or cooling coil to counteract nonlinear coil heat transfer."}, "evidence": "Since the coil output is
uniform  with steam flow, a linear valve provides a uniform change
in temperature as the proportional controller increases or
decreases the valve position to ma tch the load to maintain a tem-
perature setpoint.
An equal-percentage valve gives a very gradual change in flow
for each percentage change in valve position near the closed posi-
tion and a very rapid increase in flow near the fully open position.
An example is the control of a hydr onic heating or cooling coil. The
nonlinear coil heat transfer characte ristic (Figure 5-11a) is counter-
acted by the equal-percentage valv e characteristic (Figure 5-11b),
resulting in a nearly linear coil output.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_5bca6143eb0a08da", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Valve coefficient Cv definition", "summary": "Cv coefficient is flow in gpm through a wide-open valve at 1 psi pressure drop.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Cv_coefficient", "title": "Valve coefficient Cv definition", "summary": "Cv coefficient is flow in gpm through a wide-open valve at 1 psi pressure drop."}, "evidence": "The flow-versus-valve-posi-
tion characteristics (Figure 5-11c) assist the controller in matching
the flow to the load and minimizing coil discharge fluctuations.
5.6.3 Valve Sizing
Sizing of control valves should be undertaken by determining
the Cv (valve coefficient) required to provide the design flow condi-
tions at full load, i.e., in the wide-open position. Manufacturers fur-
nish values of Cv coefficients for each valve size. By definition, the
Cv coefficient is the flow (in gpm) through a wide-open valve at a
1 psi pressure drop across the valve [or SI equivalents]. For water
(with a specific gravity of 1), this reduces to
Q = Cv (Δp)0.5, (5-11)
where
Q = flow rate, gpm (m3/s);
Cv= required valve coefficient; and
Δp= pressure drop across valve, psi (Pa).
For steam, this formula becomes
Ws = 2.1 Cv [(p1 + p2) (p1 – p2)]0.5, (5-12)
where
Cv= required valve coefficient;
p1= valve inlet pressure, psia;.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_994b9501a8e3564f", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Water flow formula through valve", "summary": "For water, flow Q = Cv (Δp)^0.5.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "water_flow_formula", "title": "Water flow formula through valve", "summary": "For water, flow Q = Cv (Δp)^0.5."}, "evidence": "The flow-versus-valve-posi-

Ws = 2.1 Cv [(p1 + p2) (p1 – p2)]0.5, (5-12)
where
Cv= required valve coefficient;
p1= valve inlet pressure, psia;.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_923cce2ad20cdb2d", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Thermostat location guidelines", "summary": "Room thermostats should not be on exterior walls, dead corners, or unsuitable locations; best locations provide air motion and sense average temperature.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Thermostat location guidelines", "summary": "Room thermostats should not be on exterior walls, dead corners, or unsuitable locations; best locations provide air motion and sense average temperature."}, "evidence": "An alternative is to install temperature sensors with a slider
bar that permits local adjustment. The slider bar can be programmed
to establish how much temperature adjustment is allowed. The tem-
perature control profile for the V A V dampers and heating coil (or
baseboard heaters) should be as shown in Figures 10-7 and 10-8.
Room thermostats should not be located on exterior walls, in
dead corners, or in other unsuitable locations where they are likely
to be unduly affected by equipment,  occupant, or solar radiation
loads. The best locations are those that provide some air motion and
allow thermostats to sense the average room temperature. The loca-
tion of all thermostats should be in dicated on the plans. If add-on,
tamperproof covers are used, they should be constructed to allow
free air movement over the thermostat.", "source_pages": [4], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_1c134618617bbe42", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Security enclosure airflow requirement", "summary": "Metal or plastic box-type security enclosures with minimal air slits should not be used as they restrict airflow.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Security enclosure airflow requirement", "summary": "Metal or plastic box-type security enclosures with minimal air slits should not be used as they restrict airflow."}, "evidence": "Metal or plastic box-type
security enclosures with minimal ai r slits should not be used, since
they do not provide enough airflow over the thermostat for it to
respond rapidly to changing room conditions. If thermostats must be
installed on exterior walls, insula ting bases should be used and the
wall penetration sealed airtight.
10.5.2 Single-Zone, Constant-Volume Air Systems
Very small single-zone air-ha ndling units are thermodynami-
cally equivalent to room fan-coil units or residential air-condition-
ing systems and can be controlled by  simple two-position electric or
electronic thermostat/valve systems common in this type of unit.
For larger single-zone systems, one of the schemes illustrated
in Figures 10-9 and 10-11 can be used. Figure 10-9 shows a mini-
mum configuration, which may provide inadequate control and
should be used only on closely coupled systems, i.e., systems with.", "source_pages": [4], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_c1e7bb4fa5b1fcbf", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Thermostat on exterior wall insulation", "summary": "If thermostats must be on exterior walls, insulating bases should be used and wall penetration sealed airtight.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Thermostat on exterior wall insulation", "summary": "If thermostats must be on exterior walls, insulating bases should be used and wall penetration sealed airtight."}, "evidence": "Metal or plastic box-type
security enclosures with minimal ai r slits should not be used, since
they do not provide enough airflow over the thermostat for it to
respond rapidly to changing room conditions. If thermostats must be
installed on exterior walls, insula ting bases should be used and the
wall penetration sealed airtight.
10.5.2 Single-Zone, Constant-Volume Air Systems

cally equivalent to room fan-coil units or residential air-condition-
